title: Security+ Study Guide
description: High-yield domain notes, exam traps, command references, Nmap flags, and port numbers for Security+ review.
sources: Cyberkraft Security+ Ports and Protocols Reference Sheet SY0-701

domains.focus: Controls, core models, physical security, and cryptography
examTips: Categories describe where a control lives: technical, managerial, operational, or physical.
Types describe what the control does: preventive, detective, corrective, deterrent, compensating, or directive.
CIA, AAA, and Zero Trust show up heavily in scenario wording.
Honeypots and honeynets are deception and detection controls, not preventive controls.

mustKnow.title: CIA Triad
mustKnow.detail: Confidentiality limits access, integrity prevents unauthorized changes, and availability keeps resources reachable.

mustKnow.title: AAA
mustKnow.detail: Authentication proves identity, authorization defines allowed actions, and accounting records activity.

mustKnow.title: Zero Trust
mustKnow.detail: Eliminates implicit trust, continuously verifies identity and context, and enforces least privilege through policy.

mustKnow.title: Physical Controls
mustKnow.detail: Bollards, lighting, badges, sensors, guards, access control vestibules, fencing, and CCTV reduce physical intrusion risk.

mustKnow.title: Cryptography
mustKnow.detail: Symmetric encryption is fast with one shared key; asymmetric encryption uses public and private keys; hashing verifies integrity and supports signatures.

mustKnow.title: Hashing and Tokens
mustKnow.detail: Know the difference between hashing, salting, key stretching, tokenization, and authentication tokens; these are common Security+ scenario terms.

domains.focus: Threat actors, attack surfaces, social engineering, malware, and vulnerability management
examTips: Match the attacker motivation to the actor: nation-state, organized crime, insider threat, hacktivist, or script kiddie.
Do not confuse vulnerability scanning with penetration testing; scanning finds issues, testing validates exploitability.
Phishing terms are scenario-heavy: spear phishing is targeted, whaling targets executives, and vishing uses voice.
Patching, segmentation, secure configuration, and least privilege are frequent mitigation answers.

mustKnow.title: Threat Intelligence
mustKnow.detail: Indicators of compromise, tactics, techniques, procedures, reputation data, and threat feeds support detection and response.

mustKnow.title: Malware
mustKnow.detail: Viruses attach to files, worms self-propagate, trojans hide malicious behavior, ransomware encrypts data, and spyware collects information.

mustKnow.title: Social Engineering
mustKnow.detail: Pretexting, baiting, shoulder surfing, tailgating, phishing, smishing, and vishing exploit human trust.

mustKnow.title: Vulnerability Management
mustKnow.detail: Identify, analyze, prioritize, remediate, and validate vulnerabilities with risk-based urgency.

mustKnow.title: Application Weaknesses
mustKnow.detail: Injection, XSS, insecure design, insecure components, weak authentication, and poor input validation create exploitable paths.

domains.focus: Secure enterprise design, cloud models, resilience, and application security
examTips: Know the cloud responsibility split: the provider secures the cloud, the customer secures what they put in it.
Segmentation, DMZs, network access control, firewalls, IDS/IPS, and proxies are common architecture answers.
Hot, warm, and cold sites trade cost against recovery speed.
RTO is how long recovery can take; RPO is how much data loss is acceptable.

mustKnow.title: Network Zones
mustKnow.detail: DMZs expose public services while limiting access to internal networks; segmentation limits lateral movement.

mustKnow.title: Cloud Models
mustKnow.detail: IaaS gives the customer more control, SaaS gives the provider more responsibility, and PaaS sits between them.

mustKnow.title: Resiliency
mustKnow.detail: Load balancing, clustering, redundancy, backups, snapshots, replication, and failover improve availability.

mustKnow.title: Secure Design
mustKnow.detail: Least privilege, secure defaults, defense in depth, input validation, and secure SDLC reduce attack paths.

mustKnow.title: Data Protection
mustKnow.detail: Classify data, encrypt sensitive data, mask or tokenize where appropriate, and control storage and retention.

domains.focus: Identity, hardening, monitoring, incident response, and operational tooling
examTips: Least privilege and role-based access are default answers when access is too broad.
SIEM collects and correlates logs; SOAR automates response workflows.
EDR runs on endpoints; NDR watches network behavior; XDR correlates across multiple control planes.
Incident response order is preparation, detection, analysis, containment, eradication, recovery, and lessons learned.

mustKnow.title: Identity Controls
mustKnow.detail: MFA, SSO, federation, privileged access management, conditional access, and account reviews reduce identity risk.

mustKnow.title: Hardening
mustKnow.detail: Disable unnecessary services, remove default accounts, patch systems, apply secure baselines, and use allow lists.

mustKnow.title: Monitoring
mustKnow.detail: Logs, alerts, dashboards, file integrity monitoring, and behavioral analytics provide operational visibility.

mustKnow.title: Incident Response
mustKnow.detail: Containment limits impact, eradication removes the cause, recovery restores operations, and lessons learned improves future response.

mustKnow.title: Forensics
mustKnow.detail: Preserve evidence, maintain chain of custody, collect volatile data first when needed, and avoid changing evidence.

domains.focus: Governance, risk, compliance, third parties, audits, and awareness
examTips: Policies state management intent; standards define requirements; procedures provide step-by-step execution.
Qualitative risk uses descriptive ratings; quantitative risk assigns money values such as SLE and ALE.
Risk acceptance means leadership knowingly allows the risk; risk transference shifts financial impact.
Third-party risk requires due diligence, contracts, monitoring, and offboarding.

mustKnow.title: Risk Management
mustKnow.detail: Identify assets and threats, assess likelihood and impact, choose a response, and monitor residual risk.

mustKnow.title: Governance Documents
mustKnow.detail: Policies, standards, procedures, baselines, playbooks, and acceptable use rules direct consistent security behavior.

mustKnow.title: Compliance
mustKnow.detail: Frameworks and regulations drive control requirements, audit evidence, data handling, reporting, and retention.

mustKnow.title: Change Management
mustKnow.detail: Approvals, impact analysis, test results, maintenance windows, and backout plans reduce deployment risk.

mustKnow.title: Training
mustKnow.detail: Awareness programs target phishing, password hygiene, reporting, physical security, data handling, and role-specific risks.

cheatSheets.title: Security Control Categories and Types
cheatSheets.overview: Security+ expects you to classify controls two ways. The category explains where the control lives or who operates it: technical, managerial, operational, or physical. The type explains the control's purpose: prevent, deter, detect, correct, compensate, or direct behavior.

items.name: Category vs. type
items.use: A category tells where the control is applied or who manages it. A type tells what the control is intended to do.

items.name: Technical control
items.use: Security enforced by systems or tools, such as firewalls, encryption, MFA, antivirus, ACLs, IDS/IPS, and EDR.

items.name: Managerial control
items.use: Security directed by management decisions, such as policies, risk assessments, audits, vendor reviews, and security planning.

items.name: Operational control
items.use: Security performed through daily procedures, such as incident response, change management, backups, log review, and awareness training.

items.name: Physical control
items.use: Security that protects buildings, rooms, equipment, and people, such as locks, guards, fences, cameras, badges, lighting, and bollards.

items.name: Preventive control
items.value: Stops an event before it happens
items.use: Examples: firewall rule, MFA, encryption, access control vestibule, secure configuration, allow list.

items.name: Deterrent control
items.use: Examples: warning signs, visible cameras, guards, lighting, login banners, legal notices.

items.name: Detective control
items.value: Finds or alerts on an event
items.use: Examples: IDS alert, SIEM correlation, CCTV monitoring, file integrity monitoring, audit logs.

items.name: Corrective control
items.use: Examples: restoring from backup, patching, removing malware, disabling a compromised account, rebuilding a host.

items.name: Compensating control
items.use: Used when the preferred control cannot be implemented; for example, extra monitoring when a legacy system cannot support MFA.

items.name: Directive control
items.use: Examples: policies, standards, procedures, acceptable use rules, onboarding guidance, and required security training.

items.name: Exam pattern
items.use: A camera is physical by category and detective or deterrent by type. A firewall is technical by category and usually preventive by type.

cheatSheets.title: Ports and Protocols
cheatSheets.overview: Memorize the common port numbers and the service purpose. On the exam, ports usually appear in troubleshooting, firewall-rule, secure-protocol, and protocol-selection scenarios.

items.name: FTP
items.use: File transfer; port 21 controls the session and port 20 transfers data.

items.name: SSH / SFTP
items.use: Encrypted remote shell and SSH-based file transfer.

items.name: TACACS+
items.use: Cisco AAA protocol.

items.name: DNS
items.use: Maps domain names to IP addresses.

items.name: DHCP
items.use: Assigns IP configuration to clients.

items.name: HTTP / HTTPS
items.use: Web traffic; HTTPS uses TLS.

items.name: Kerberos
items.use: Ticket-based network authentication.

items.name: POP3 / POP3S
items.use: Email retrieval; POP3S is TLS-protected.

items.name: IMAP / IMAPS
items.use: Two-way email mailbox access; IMAPS is TLS-protected.

items.name: SNMP
items.use: Network monitoring and management.

items.name: LDAP / LDAPS
items.use: Directory access; LDAPS is TLS-protected.

items.name: RADIUS
items.use: AAA for network access.

items.name: RDP
items.use: Remote Windows desktop access.

items.name: Diameter
items.use: Upgrade path from RADIUS.

items.name: SRTP
items.use: Secure real-time audio/video transport.

items.name: L2TP
items.use: Layer 2 tunneling, often paired with IPSec.

items.name: PPTP
items.use: Deprecated VPN tunneling protocol.

cheatSheets.title: Linux Commands
cheatSheets.overview: These commands are useful for Security+ performance-based and operations-style questions. Focus on what each command is used for, not every possible flag.

items.name: ls / ls -al
items.use: View directory contents; -al includes hidden files and detailed metadata.

items.name: cd
items.use: Move through the filesystem; cd .. moves up and cd / moves to root.

items.name: touch
items.use: Create an empty file or update timestamps.

items.name: cat
items.use: Print file contents or concatenate files with redirection.

items.name: mv
items.use: Move files to another path or rename them.

items.name: rm
items.use: Remove a file from the filesystem.

items.name: mkdir / rmdir
items.use: Create directories or remove empty directories.

items.name: grep
items.use: Find matching text in files; -r is recursive and -i ignores case.

items.name: find
items.use: Locate files by name or other file attributes.

items.name: chown
items.use: Modify file owner or owner and group.

items.name: ssh
items.use: Open an encrypted shell to another host.

items.name: ping
items.use: Send ICMP echo requests to test connectivity.

items.name: ip a / ifconfig
items.use: Show IP addresses and network interface details.

items.name: top / ps
items.use: Inspect running processes and resource usage.

items.name: kill
items.use: Stop a process by process ID.

items.name: df / free
items.use: Review disk space and memory usage.

cheatSheets.title: Nmap Commands
cheatSheets.overview: Nmap questions usually test scan intent: host discovery, port selection, service/version detection, OS detection, timing, evasion, scripts, and output formats.

items.name: -sS
items.use: Common stealthier TCP scan.

items.name: -sT
items.use: Completes the TCP connection.

items.name: -sU
items.use: Checks UDP services.

items.name: -sn / -sP
items.use: Find live hosts without a full port scan.

items.name: -p
items.use: Specify ports to scan.

items.name: -F
items.use: Scan fewer common ports.

items.name: -sV
items.use: Identify service versions on open ports.

items.name: -O
items.use: Attempt to identify operating systems.

items.name: -A
items.use: Enable OS detection, version detection, scripts, and traceroute.

items.name: -T<0-5>
items.use: Adjust scan speed and aggressiveness.

items.name: --min-rate / --max-rate
items.use: Control minimum and maximum packet send rate.

items.name: -f
items.use: Split packets for firewall or IDS evasion testing.

items.name: -D
items.use: Make scan traffic appear to come from multiple sources.

items.name: -S
items.use: Set a source address for scan packets.

items.name: --script
items.use: Run a specific Nmap Scripting Engine script.

items.name: --open
items.use: Show only open ports.

items.name: -oA
items.use: Save normal, XML, and grepable output with one basename.

items.name: --packet-trace
items.use: Show packets sent and received.

cheatSheets.title: Hashing, Salting, and Tokens
cheatSheets.overview: This section separates similar-looking crypto and identity terms. Hashing verifies integrity and supports password storage, salting and key stretching harden passwords, tokenization replaces sensitive data, and authentication tokens prove possession.

items.name: Hashing
items.use: Transforms data into a fixed-length value used for integrity checks, password verification, and digital signatures.

items.name: Message digest
items.use: A compact summary of data; if the data changes, the digest should change.

items.name: Collision
items.use: A weakness where two different inputs produce the same hash; collisions can lead to algorithm deprecation.

items.name: SHA-256
items.use: Modern secure hashing choice for integrity checks and many Security+ examples.

items.name: SHA-3
items.use: Secure hash family with variants similar in output length to SHA-2.

items.name: SHA-1
items.use: Older hash algorithm; know it is weak for collision resistance and should not be chosen for new security designs.

items.name: MD5
items.use: Older fast hash; still appears in legacy checks and forensics examples, but is not secure for modern cryptographic trust.

items.name: Salt
items.use: Added before hashing so identical passwords produce different hashes and rainbow tables are much harder to use.

items.name: Pepper
items.use: Additional secret data added before hashing; unlike a salt, it should be protected separately from the password database.

items.name: Key stretching
items.use: Runs passwords through many hashing iterations to slow offline cracking attempts.

items.name: PBKDF2
items.use: Key stretching algorithm that uses many iterations of salting and hashing to derive stronger keys from passwords.

items.name: bcrypt
items.use: Adaptive password-hashing algorithm designed to slow brute-force cracking.

items.name: Argon2
items.use: Modern password-hashing algorithm built to resist GPU and memory-based cracking advantages.

items.name: HMAC
items.use: Combines a hash function with a shared secret key to verify message integrity and authenticity.

items.name: Tokenization
items.use: Replaces sensitive values with non-sensitive tokens that map back through a lookup system; unlike hashing, it is designed to be reversible by authorized systems.
clues: Replace sensitive data with lookup value

items.name: Data masking
items.use: Obscures part of a sensitive value, such as showing only the last four digits.

items.name: Hard token
items.use: A hardware device, smartcard, USB key, or fob used as a possession factor.

items.name: Soft token
items.use: An app-based token generator, commonly used for one-time passwords.

items.name: HOTP
items.use: One-time password generated from a shared seed and a counter or event.

items.name: TOTP
items.use: One-time password generated from a shared seed and the current time window.

items.name: Honeytoken
items.use: Fake credential or data item planted to detect misuse when accessed or exfiltrated.

items.name: Pass-the-hash
items.use: Attack where a captured password hash, often NTLM, is reused to authenticate without knowing the plaintext password.

cheatSheets.title: Change Management and PKI
cheatSheets.overview: Change management reduces the risk of production changes, while PKI supports trust, certificates, encryption, and identity validation. Scenario questions often ask which process or certificate mechanism best reduces a specific risk.

items.name: Approval process
items.use: Requires review and sign-off before a change is implemented.

items.name: Impact analysis
items.use: Identifies affected systems, users, security controls, and business processes before implementation.

items.name: Backout plan
items.use: Defines how to restore the prior state if a change fails or creates instability.

items.name: Maintenance window
items.use: Planned period for updates, outages, restarts, and validation.

items.name: Dependencies
items.use: Systems, applications, or libraries that may break when a related component changes.

items.name: Version control
items.use: Records configuration, code, and document changes for accountability and rollback.

items.name: TPM
items.use: Hardware chip that stores cryptographic material and supports platform integrity.

items.name: HSM
items.use: Dedicated device for secure key storage and high-volume cryptographic operations.

items.name: KMS
items.use: Central service for creating, storing, rotating, and controlling encryption keys.

items.name: Secure enclave
items.use: Protects sensitive operations and secrets away from the main operating system.

items.name: CRL
items.use: Published list of revoked certificates.

items.name: OCSP
items.use: Checks certificate revocation status in near real time.

items.name: Root of trust
items.use: Highest trusted authority or component used to validate a chain of trust.

items.name: CSR
items.use: Request sent to a CA containing public key and identity information for certificate issuance.

items.name: Wildcard certificate
items.use: Certificate that secures multiple subdomains under the same domain.

cheatSheets.title: Threat Vectors, Vulnerabilities, and Attacks
cheatSheets.overview: This tab covers how attacks enter an environment, what weaknesses they exploit, and what attack patterns look like. Use it to distinguish vulnerability types from indicators of malicious activity.

items.name: Attack surface
items.use: All places an attacker can try to enter or affect a system.

items.name: Default credentials
items.use: Common weakness when default usernames or passwords are not changed.

items.name: Unsupported system
items.use: System or application that no longer receives security updates.

items.name: Supply chain
items.use: Risk introduced through vendors, suppliers, MSPs, software providers, or hardware providers.

items.name: Typosquatting
items.use: Uses misspelled or similar domains to capture users or deliver attacks.

items.name: Watering hole
items.use: Targets victims by infecting a site they are likely to visit.

items.name: Brand impersonation
items.use: Uses a known company identity to make phishing or fraud more believable.

items.name: Buffer overflow
items.use: Application flaw where input exceeds memory boundaries and may allow code execution.

items.name: Race condition
items.use: Security issue caused by unexpected ordering between check and use operations.

items.name: VM escape
items.use: Attack where code escapes a guest VM to affect the host or other guests.

items.name: Jailbreaking
items.use: Removes OS protections on a mobile device and increases security risk.

items.name: Side loading
items.use: Installing apps from outside the official app store or approved channel.

items.name: Downgrade attack
items.use: Attempts to make systems use a less secure protocol or cryptographic mode.

items.name: Birthday attack
items.use: Uses collision probability to attack hash functions faster than brute force.

items.name: Directory traversal
items.use: Attempts to access files outside the intended directory.

items.name: Replay attack
items.use: Resends captured authentication or transaction data to gain unauthorized access.

items.name: RFID cloning
items.use: Copies RFID credential data to impersonate an access token.

items.name: DDoS amplification
items.use: Uses third-party services to multiply attack traffic against a target.

cheatSheets.title: Threat Actors and Motivations
cheatSheets.overview: Actor questions usually hinge on motive, resources, and sophistication. Match clues like espionage, financial gain, revenge, ideology, or chaos to the most likely attacker type.

items.name: Nation-state
items.use: Usually highly funded and sophisticated; common motivations include espionage, disruption, war, and strategic advantage.

items.name: Organized crime
items.use: Usually financially motivated; common goals include fraud, ransomware, data theft, extortion, and credential resale.

items.name: Insider threat
items.use: May be malicious or unintentional; motivations include revenge, financial gain, negligence, or pressure from another actor.

items.name: Hacktivist
items.use: Motivated by political, philosophical, ethical, or social beliefs; often uses defacement, leaks, or disruption.

items.name: Unskilled attacker
items.use: Uses existing tools or scripts with limited sophistication; often motivated by curiosity, chaos, reputation, or easy targets.

items.name: Shadow IT
items.use: Business users deploy tools or services outside IT approval, creating unmanaged risk.

items.name: Internal vs. external
items.use: Internal actors already have some trusted access; external actors attack from outside the organization.

items.name: Resources and funding
items.use: Higher funding usually means better tools, persistence, infrastructure, and ability to run long campaigns.

items.name: Sophistication
items.use: Exam scenarios often hint at sophistication through stealth, custom malware, persistence, or use of public tools.

items.name: Data exfiltration
items.use: Common motivation for nation-states, insiders, and organized crime.

items.name: Financial gain
items.use: Strongly associated with organized crime, ransomware, fraud, and credential theft.

items.name: Espionage
items.use: Strongly associated with nation-states and advanced persistent threats.

items.name: Disruption/chaos
items.use: Can indicate hacktivists, nation-states, or unskilled attackers depending on the scenario.

items.name: Revenge
items.use: Often points to an insider threat, especially a disgruntled employee or former contractor.

cheatSheets.title: Malware Types and Indicators
cheatSheets.overview: Malware questions test how malicious software behaves and what evidence it leaves behind. Learn the difference between propagation, concealment, credential theft, remote control, persistence, and destructive impact.

items.name: Malware
items.use: Any software designed to harm systems, steal data, evade controls, disrupt service, or give an attacker unauthorized access.

items.name: Virus
items.use: Malware that attaches to a host file or program and usually needs user action or execution to spread.

items.name: Worm
items.use: Spreads across systems or networks without needing to attach to a host file.

items.name: Trojan
items.use: Appears legitimate but performs malicious actions after installation or execution.

items.name: Ransomware
items.use: Blocks access to files or systems and demands payment, often combined with data theft or extortion.

items.name: Spyware
items.use: Collects user activity, credentials, browsing, screenshots, or other information without consent.

items.name: Bloatware
items.use: Consumes resources, expands attack surface, or creates privacy and update risks.

items.name: Keylogger
items.use: Records typed input to steal passwords, messages, payment data, or sensitive content.

items.name: Logic bomb
items.use: Executes malicious actions when a condition is met, such as a date, event, or account change.

items.name: Rootkit
items.use: Hides processes, files, accounts, or attacker access, often at a low system level.

items.name: Backdoor
items.use: Bypasses normal authentication or controls so an attacker can return later.

items.name: Botnet
items.use: Group of compromised systems controlled by an attacker for DDoS, spam, mining, or credential attacks.

items.name: Remote access trojan
items.use: Trojan that gives an attacker remote control over a compromised host.

items.name: Fileless malware
items.use: Uses legitimate tools or memory-resident techniques to reduce files written to disk.

items.name: Potentially unwanted program
items.use: Software that may be unwanted, intrusive, risky, or bundled even if not always strictly malicious.

items.name: Signature-based detection
items.use: Finds malware by matching known hashes, byte patterns, behaviors, or indicators.

items.name: Behavior-based detection
items.use: Finds malware by observing actions such as persistence, privilege abuse, encryption bursts, or command-and-control traffic.

items.name: Sandbox analysis
items.use: Runs suspicious files in a controlled environment to observe behavior safely.

items.name: Command and control
items.use: Attacker-controlled communication channel used to send instructions or receive stolen data.

items.name: Persistence
items.use: Techniques that keep malware active after restart or user logout.

items.name: Resource consumption
items.use: High CPU, memory, disk, bandwidth, or battery use can indicate malware activity.

items.name: Blocked content
items.use: Web, email, DNS, or endpoint blocks can signal attempted malware delivery or callback.

items.name: Missing logs
items.use: Deleted or absent logs may indicate malware or attacker cleanup activity.

items.name: Out-of-cycle logging
items.use: Events at unexpected times can indicate automated malware activity or unauthorized access.

cheatSheets.title: Scanning, Testing, Phishing, and Mitigation
cheatSheets.overview: This section connects assessment methods, social engineering terms, and common mitigation choices. The exam often contrasts scanning with exploitation and asks for the most practical next defensive action.

items.name: Vulnerability scan
items.use: Automated identification of missing patches, misconfigurations, exposed services, weak settings, or known CVEs.

items.name: Penetration test
items.use: Authorized attempt to exploit weaknesses and prove business impact.

items.name: Vulnerability assessment
items.use: Reviews scan findings, context, risk, and remediation priority.

items.name: Responsible disclosure
items.use: Researcher reports a vulnerability privately so the owner can fix it before public release.

items.name: Bug bounty
items.use: Program that pays or recognizes researchers for valid vulnerability reports.

items.name: Phishing
items.use: Generic attempt to trick users through email, chat, links, attachments, or fake login pages.

items.name: Spear phishing
items.use: Customized attack against a specific person, group, or role.

items.name: Whaling
items.use: Targets high-value leaders such as executives, finance heads, or privileged decision makers.

items.name: Vishing
items.use: Uses phone calls or voice messages to trick victims.

items.name: Smishing
items.use: Uses text messages to trick victims into clicking, calling, or sharing information.

items.name: Business email compromise
items.use: Tricks users into payments, wire transfers, gift cards, or sensitive data release.

items.name: Pretexting
items.use: Creates a believable story to make the victim reveal information or perform an action.

items.name: Impersonation
items.use: Attacker poses as a user, executive, help desk worker, vendor, or authority figure.

items.name: Patching
items.use: Applies vendor updates to remediate vulnerabilities.

items.name: Segmentation
items.use: Divides networks or systems so compromise does not spread easily.

items.name: Secure configuration
items.use: Uses secure baselines, disables weak defaults, and enforces approved settings.

items.name: Least privilege
items.use: Limits users, services, and admins to only the permissions needed.

items.name: Allow list
items.use: Permits known-good apps, addresses, services, or actions.

items.name: Disable unused ports/protocols
items.use: Turns off unnecessary services so attackers have fewer entry points.

items.name: Default password changes
items.use: Prevents common attacks against known vendor usernames and passwords.

cheatSheets.title: Security Architecture Models
cheatSheets.overview: Architecture questions ask you to choose or interpret secure designs across cloud, virtualization, networking, industrial systems, and access patterns. Pay attention to placement, isolation, availability, and control selection.

items.name: IaC
items.use: Defines and deploys infrastructure through versioned code or templates.

items.name: Serverless
items.use: Runs code without managing servers; security focus shifts to permissions, code, events, and configuration.

items.name: Microservices
items.use: Improves scalability but increases API, identity, and network security requirements.

items.name: Containerization
items.use: Packages applications and dependencies; requires image scanning and runtime isolation.

items.name: Virtualization
items.use: Runs multiple guest systems on shared hardware; isolation and hypervisor security matter.

items.name: IoT
items.use: Embedded network devices that often need segmentation, secure defaults, and patch planning.

items.name: ICS/SCADA
items.use: Operational technology environments where availability and safety are major concerns.

items.name: RTOS
items.use: Operating system for time-sensitive embedded or industrial systems.

items.name: Air gap
items.use: Separates a system or network from other networks for high-security isolation.

items.name: SDN
items.use: Separates network control from forwarding for programmable network management.

items.name: SD-WAN
items.use: Centralizes WAN connectivity policy, routing, and security across sites.

items.name: SASE
items.use: Combines network access and cloud-delivered security services.

items.name: WAF
items.use: Filters HTTP traffic to protect web applications from attacks like injection and XSS.

items.name: UTM
items.use: Appliance or service combining multiple security functions.

items.name: NGFW
items.use: Firewall with application awareness, identity awareness, and advanced inspection features.

items.name: Jump server
items.use: Intermediate system used to administer secure network segments.

items.name: Fail-open
items.use: Maintains availability during control failure but may reduce security.

items.name: Fail-closed
items.use: Maintains security during control failure but may reduce availability.

cheatSheets.title: Data Classification and Protection
cheatSheets.overview: Data questions test what kind of data you are protecting, where it is in its lifecycle, and which protection method fits the scenario. Classification drives handling, access, storage, retention, and legal requirements.

items.name: Regulated data
items.use: Data subject to laws or regulations, such as health, payment, or privacy records.

items.name: Trade secret
items.use: Information with business value that must be protected from disclosure.

items.name: Intellectual property
items.use: Patents, source code, designs, formulas, and proprietary content.

items.name: Sensitive
items.use: Data that could harm people or the organization if disclosed.

items.name: Confidential
items.use: Information limited to approved users or groups.

items.name: Private
items.use: Data not intended for public disclosure.

items.name: Critical
items.use: Data required for core operations or mission functions.

items.name: Data at rest
items.use: Protect with storage controls such as encryption, permissions, and backups.

items.name: Data in transit
items.use: Protect with TLS, VPNs, IPsec, secure protocols, and certificate validation.

items.name: Data in use
items.use: Protect with access controls, secure enclaves, isolation, and application controls.

items.name: Data sovereignty
items.use: Data must follow laws of the country or region where it is stored or processed.

items.name: Geographic restrictions
items.use: Restricts where data or services may be stored, accessed, or processed.

items.name: Segmentation
items.use: Separates sensitive data and systems to limit access and blast radius.

items.name: Permission restrictions
items.use: Uses authorization controls to restrict who can read, write, or manage data.

cheatSheets.title: Security Operations and IAM
cheatSheets.overview: Operations and identity are the largest practical part of Security+. This section covers secure baselines, mobile and wireless management, asset lifecycle, account lifecycle, access models, and privileged access.

items.name: Secure baseline
items.use: Standard hardened configuration that is established, deployed, and maintained.

items.name: Site survey
items.use: Evaluates wireless placement, coverage, interference, and security requirements.

items.name: Heat map
items.use: Visualizes wireless coverage and dead zones.

items.name: WPA3
items.use: Current wireless security standard with stronger authentication and encryption protections.

items.name: MDM
items.use: Manages mobile configuration, compliance, inventory, and remote actions.

items.name: BYOD
items.use: Personal device used for work; increases policy, privacy, and control complexity.

items.name: COPE
items.use: Company-owned device that allows personal use under organizational control.

items.name: CYOD
items.use: User selects from approved company-supported devices.

items.name: Asset inventory
items.use: Records hardware, software, ownership, classification, and lifecycle state.

items.name: Enumeration
items.use: Identifies systems, services, users, shares, and other resources.

items.name: Sanitization
items.use: Removes data from media before reuse or disposal.

items.name: Destruction
items.use: Destroys media so data cannot be recovered.

items.name: Data retention
items.use: Defines how long data is stored and when it must be disposed.

items.name: Provisioning
items.use: Creates accounts and grants needed access for users or services.

items.name: Deprovisioning
items.use: Disables accounts and removes access when no longer needed.

items.name: Identity proofing
items.use: Confirms a person is who they claim to be before issuing credentials.

items.name: SAML
items.use: XML-based protocol commonly used for enterprise SSO.

items.name: LDAP
items.use: Accesses and manages directory information.

items.name: OAuth
items.use: Allows third-party access without sharing the user's password.
clues: Third-party app access without password sharing

items.name: Attestation
items.use: Provides proof about identity, device state, or compliance.

items.name: PAM
items.use: Controls administrative access with vaulting, approvals, monitoring, and session limits.

items.name: JIT permissions
items.use: Grants elevated access only when needed and only for a limited time.

items.name: Ephemeral credentials
items.use: Temporary credentials that reduce risk from credential theft.

cheatSheets.title: Vulnerability Management and Monitoring
cheatSheets.overview: Vulnerability management turns findings into prioritized remediation. Monitoring turns logs, network data, alerts, and control signals into visibility for detection, response, and reporting.

items.name: Static analysis
items.use: Finds flaws by analyzing source code or binaries.

items.name: Dynamic analysis
items.use: Finds flaws by testing a running application.

items.name: Package monitoring
items.use: Watches libraries and packages for vulnerable versions.

items.name: OSINT
items.use: Collects public information to support security decisions.

items.name: Threat feed
items.use: Provides external information about malicious IPs, domains, hashes, tactics, or vulnerabilities.

items.name: Dark web
items.use: May reveal leaked credentials, exploit sales, or threat actor activity.

items.name: Responsible disclosure
items.use: Allows vulnerabilities to be reported and fixed before public disclosure.

items.name: Bug bounty
items.use: Incentivizes external researchers to report valid vulnerabilities.

items.name: CVSS
items.use: Common Vulnerability Scoring System used to prioritize vulnerabilities.

items.name: CVE
items.use: Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures identifier for a known flaw.

items.name: False positive
items.use: Tool reports a problem that is not actually present.
clues: Tool says issue exists but it does not

items.name: False negative
items.use: Tool fails to report a real problem.

items.name: Exposure factor
items.use: Estimated percentage of asset value lost in a specific event.

items.name: Compensating control
items.use: Reduces risk when the preferred control cannot be implemented.

items.name: Exception
items.use: Formal approval to operate outside a requirement for a defined reason or time.

items.name: SCAP
items.use: Standardizes vulnerability and configuration data for automated assessment.

items.name: Benchmark
items.use: Defines a target secure configuration to compare systems against.

items.name: DLP
items.use: Detects and blocks unauthorized sensitive data movement.

items.name: SNMP trap
items.use: Network device notification sent to a monitoring system.

items.name: NetFlow
items.use: Summarizes network flow information for monitoring and investigation.

items.name: DMARC/DKIM/SPF
items.use: Controls that reduce email spoofing and improve domain sender validation.

items.name: FIM
items.use: Detects unauthorized changes to important files.

items.name: NAC
items.use: Checks device or user compliance before allowing network access.

items.name: UBA
items.use: Finds anomalous behavior that may indicate compromise or insider risk.

cheatSheets.title: Automation, Incident Response, and Investigations
cheatSheets.overview: Automation improves speed and consistency, but can add complexity or single points of failure. Incident response and investigations focus on process order, evidence handling, root cause, and reliable data sources.

items.name: Guard rails
items.use: Prevent unsafe actions or enforce policy during provisioning and operations.

items.name: Ticket creation
items.use: Automatically opens work items for security events or operational tasks.

items.name: Escalation
items.use: Moves incidents or alerts to higher support or authority when thresholds are met.

items.name: CI testing
items.use: Runs security and quality checks during continuous integration.

items.name: API integration
items.use: Allows security tools and workflows to exchange data and actions.

items.name: Single point of failure
items.use: Risk introduced when automation or infrastructure depends on one critical part.

items.name: Technical debt
items.use: Complexity or shortcuts that increase future maintenance and security risk.

items.name: Root cause analysis
items.use: Determines why an incident or failure happened so it can be prevented.

items.name: Threat hunting
items.use: Looks for hidden threats before alerts clearly identify them.

items.name: Legal hold
items.use: Stops deletion or alteration of data relevant to legal matters.

items.name: E-discovery
items.use: Identifies and produces electronic records for legal processes.

items.name: Chain of custody
items.use: Documents who handled evidence, when, and why.

items.name: Acquisition
items.use: Collects forensic copies, logs, packet captures, metadata, or endpoint data.

items.name: Packet capture
items.use: Records network packets for investigation and troubleshooting.

items.name: Metadata
items.use: Supports investigations with timestamps, owners, paths, headers, and other context.

cheatSheets.title: Governance, Risk, Compliance, and Third Parties
cheatSheets.overview: GRC questions test policy hierarchy, roles, risk math, risk decisions, vendor oversight, compliance duties, and assessment types. Read scenarios for who owns the risk and what evidence or agreement is needed.

items.name: AUP
items.use: Defines allowed and prohibited use of organizational systems.

items.name: Playbook
items.use: Step-by-step operational response for a known scenario.

items.name: Governance structure
items.use: Boards, committees, government entities, or centralized/decentralized models that direct security.

items.name: Data owner
items.use: Defines requirements and accountability for data protection.

items.name: Controller
items.use: Privacy role that decides why and how personal data is processed.

items.name: Processor
items.use: Privacy role that handles data on behalf of a controller.

items.name: Custodian/steward
items.use: Implements and maintains controls for systems or data.

items.name: Risk register
items.use: Documents risks, owners, ratings, responses, and status.

items.name: KRI
items.use: Metric that signals changing risk levels.

items.name: Risk appetite
items.use: Amount and type of risk leadership is willing to pursue.

items.name: Risk threshold
items.use: Point where risk exceeds tolerance and must be escalated or treated.

items.name: BIA
items.use: Identifies critical processes, impact, RTO, RPO, MTTR, and MTBF needs.

items.name: SLE/ALE/ARO
items.use: SLE is single loss, ARO is yearly frequency, and ALE is expected yearly loss.

items.name: Due diligence
items.use: Research and validation before selecting or trusting a third party.

items.name: Right-to-audit
items.use: Allows the customer to inspect or verify vendor controls.

items.name: SLA
items.use: Defines measurable service expectations such as uptime or response time.

items.name: MOU/MOA
items.use: Documents shared understanding or responsibilities between organizations.

items.name: MSA/SOW
items.use: Defines broad contract terms and the specific work to be performed.

items.name: NDA
items.use: Protects confidential information shared with another party.

items.name: Rules of engagement
items.use: Defines scope, timing, allowed methods, and limits for tests or assessments.

items.name: Known environment test
items.use: Assessment where testers have detailed target information.

items.name: Partially known test
items.use: Assessment where testers have limited target information.

items.name: Unknown environment test
items.use: Assessment where testers start with little or no target information.

cheatSheets.title: Security Awareness
cheatSheets.overview: Awareness questions focus on changing user behavior. Know how training, reporting, phishing campaigns, remote-work guidance, and operational security reduce human-driven risk.

items.name: Phishing campaign
items.use: Tests and trains users on recognizing and reporting phishing attempts.

items.name: Suspicious message reporting
items.use: Encourages users to report suspicious emails, texts, chats, or calls quickly.

items.name: Anomalous behavior
items.use: Unexpected, risky, or unintentional actions that may indicate compromise or training needs.

items.name: Risky environment
items.use: Public, remote, travel, or shared environments that need extra caution.

items.name: Removable media and cables
items.use: USB drives and cables can introduce malware, data loss, or rogue device risk.

items.name: Hybrid/remote work
items.use: Requires training around home networks, secure access, device handling, and data protection.

items.name: Policy handbook
items.use: Documents expected behavior and security responsibilities.

items.name: Initial training
items.use: Security instruction given when users join or take on a new role.

items.name: Recurring training
items.use: Periodic reinforcement to keep users current on threats and policies.

items.name: Operational security
items.use: Avoids exposing sensitive details through conversation, screens, documents, or workflows.
